nativeRange.habitat: Ephemeral seasonal pools, water-filled depressions, and lowland floodplain swamps in the lower Pungwe and Zambezi river basins near Beira

decorPreferences: peat substrate or peat moss spawning layer

diet.notes: Micro-carnivore that tends to do best on live and frozen foods. Daphnia, brine shrimp, bloodworms, white worms, and grindal worms are commonly recommended. Has been reported to accept some frozen and dried foods but live prey is generally preferred for conditioning.

avoidWith: fast-swimming community fish that outcompete for food

imageCandidates.notes: Cisamarc, 631x474. Striking vivid live male in planted tank showing the diagnostic red body with turquoise-blue iridescent scaling, orange-red head, and red caudal with black margin. Sharp, whole fish, well-lit.
Andrew Bogott, 1388x780. Sharp studio male on light background; cleanly shows red/turquoise reticulated body and the characteristic white-and-black caudal with orange band. Live specimen.
Andreas Wretstrom, 450x342. Colorful live male on black background with red/blue barred fins; lower resolution so kept as a non-recommended extra for angle variety.

summary: An annual killifish from Mozambique's seasonal pools, considered among the most colorful freshwater fish — males display vivid red and blue iridescence. Lifespan is generally 6–12 months; eggs survive the dry season in substrate and hatch upon flooding.
careNotes:
ANNUAL SPECIES: Nothobranchius rachovii has a genetically-programmed lifespan of approximately 6–12 months, reflecting an adaptation to ephemeral seasonal pools that dry completely each year. This is not a sign of poor husbandry — the fish completes its full life cycle within one year regardless of conditions. Cooler water temperatures (closer to 20°C) are generally advised to slow the aging process slightly; warmer temperatures are known to shorten lifespan further.

ESCAPE RISK: This species is a strong jumper. A tight-fitting, fully enclosed lid is essential — gaps around equipment are sufficient for escape.

SPECIES-ONLY TANK RECOMMENDED: Best kept in a species-only setup of 30–40 L. Males tend to be aggressive toward one another; a ratio of one male to two or three females is commonly advised. Some keepers have reported success in community tanks with equally small, non-competitive species, but species-only setups reduce stress and competition for food.

BREEDING: Spawning occurs in substrate. A 5–7 cm peat moss layer is typically provided as spawning medium. After spawning, the peat is removed, semi-dried, and stored sealed at room temperature (20–25°C) for approximately 5–9 months before being re-wetted to trigger hatching. Fry grow extremely rapidly and reach sexual maturity in approximately 3–6 weeks. The killifish hobbyist community primarily trades eggs rather than live fish, as eggs tolerate shipping far better.

PLACE OF ORIGIN: Lower Pungwe and Zambezi river basins, eastern Mozambique, near Beira. Wild populations are exposed to significantly fluctuating water chemistry as pools concentrate and dilute seasonally; the species tolerates a moderately wide parameter range.

COLOR NOTE: Males are among the most brilliantly colored freshwater fish available — vivid red and blue iridescent patterns with contrasting fin markings. Females are drab brown and considerably smaller. Multiple collection strains exist (labeled with codes like 'Beira 1998' or 'MOZ 99/1'); hobbyists are generally advised to keep strains separate to avoid hybridization and preserve locality genetics.

SOFT/ACIDIC WATER PREFERRED: Peat filtration or peat extract is commonly used to soften water and lower pH slightly, mimicking the tannin-rich seasonal pools of the native habitat.
breedingNotes: The Bluefin Notho spawns in substrate — provide a 5–7 cm layer of peat moss on the tank bottom. Males court females vigorously; after spawning the pair pushes eggs into the peat. Once spawning is complete, the peat is removed, partially dried, and stored sealed at room temperature until re-wetted with aged soft water to trigger hatching. Fry are large enough to accept baby brine shrimp immediately and grow very rapidly, reaching sexual maturity in as little as 3–6 weeks. Because eggs can be stored and shipped dry, egg exchange among killifish enthusiasts is a well-established and common way to obtain this species.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided tank footprint (45×30 cm), water parameters, native range, diet, lifespan, and breeding notes.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Cyprinodontiformes / Nothobranchiidae), max size (6.0 cm TL), temperature (20–24°C), pH (6.0–7.0), hardness (4–6 dH). Confirmed native range.

additional.name: AquariumBreeder
additional.notes: Cross-checked tank size (40 L / 10 gal), pH (5.5–7.0), gH (1–10), kH (1–8), escape risk, and breeding diapause period (6–9 months). Adult size listed as 2.5–4.5 cm appears to measure females/subadults; male max 6 cm confirmed by FishBase and SeriouslyFish.

additional.name: Castle Dawn Aquatics
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (6 cm), temperature (20–23°C), escape risk (high), and compatibility notes. Hardness listed as 5–20 dGH is wider than other sources; averaged toward the tighter consensus of gH 1–10.

additional.name: LiveAquaria
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature (72–75°F / 22–24°C), pH (6.0–7.5), dKH (3–8), diet, and care level. Tank size recommendation of 20 gal (76 L) appears conservative relative to species-only specialist sources; 30–40 L consensus preferred.

fish.breedingNotes: Spawning takes place in peat substrate, with the eggs entering a resting phase — known as diapause — that requires 5 to 9 months of dry storage before they are viable for hatching. Because eggs are robust enough to survive storage and shipping, exchange of dried eggs among killifish enthusiasts is a well-established alternative to purchasing live fish.
